To determine the number of ways to arrange three indistinguishable rooks on a $6 \times 6$ board such that no two rooks attack each other, we must ensure that no two rooks share the same row or the same column.

First, consider the rooks as distinguishable to simplify the selection process. We need to choose 3 distinct rows out of the 6 available rows. The number of ways to do this is given by the binomial coefficient $\binom{6}{3}$.
$$ \binom{6}{3} = \frac{6 \times 5 \times 4}{3 \times 2 \times 1} = 20 $$

Next, we need to choose 3 distinct columns out of the 6 available columns. Similarly, the number of ways to choose the columns is $\binom{6}{3}$, which is also 20.

Once we have selected 3 specific rows and 3 specific columns, we have a $3 \times 3$ subgrid formed by their intersections. We must place one rook in each of the 3 chosen rows such that they also occupy the 3 chosen columns without any two sharing a column. If the rooks were distinguishable, there would be $3!$ (which is 6) ways to assign the chosen columns to the chosen rows.

However, the problem states that the rooks are indistinguishable. This means that the order in which we place the rooks does not matter. When we select a set of 3 rows and a set of 3 columns, there is only one way to place 3 indistinguishable rooks on the intersection points such that every selected row and every selected column contains exactly one rook.
